sourceMovie.title: Salt
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Salt (2010) stuck because it commits to its action premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Phillip Noyce's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The action and mystery blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: The Bourne Ultimatum
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Bourne Ultimatum (2007) captures the action appeal of Salt without simply imitating it. The mystery streak running through both films is what puts The Bourne Ultimatum at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: In the Line of Fire
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The action appeal of Salt — that dark edge, the mystery — translates cleanly into In the Line of Fire (1993). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Conspiracy Theory
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Conspiracy Theory (1997) clearly understood what makes action work — the same quality that keeps Salt rewatchable. Expect the same tense rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Mission: Impossible - Ghost Protocol
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Mission: Impossible - Ghost Protocol (2011) is the kind of film Salt fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The action overlap is real, but it's the tense emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: RED
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: RED shares Salt's gift for action — the 2010 film delivers thriller without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: The Equalizer 2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Salt compelling — the action, the thriller — shows up in The Equalizer 2 (2018) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that dark storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Spy Game
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The action energy of Salt is alive in Spy Game (2001), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a dark payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Shoot 'Em Up
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Shoot 'Em Up (2007) operates in the same action as Salt, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The funny payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Ransom
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Ransom (1996) belongs to the same conversation as Salt — both anchor their action in thriller emotion that doesn't let go. The tense undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: The Sum of All Fears
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Salt's action stayed with you, The Sum of All Fears (2002) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The thriller through-line is the real common thread between them.

faq.question: What should I watch after Salt?
faq.answer: Try The Bourne Ultimatum, In the Line of Fire, Conspiracy Theory—each shares DNA with Salt while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Salt?
faq.answer: Salt (2010) is best described as Action, Mystery, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Salt worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 6.4/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this action lane.
